questions: A 2015 open-access textbook on criminal law for undergraduate students was published by University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing. Using that publisher as your filter, identify the information technology–focused book in the computer science field released by the same publisher. Provide its title, copyright year, license type, intended academic audience, and review count. In addition, explain the process offered by the hosting platform for suggesting corrections or additions to a book’s bibliographic record, including who should be contacted for content-related improvements and where to direct general questions.

Identify the open-access undergraduate criminal law textbook published in 2015 by a Midwestern university library press that uses an “applied approach” to break down crimes into component elements and includes 14 chapters ending with an appendix of case listings. What are its ISBN-13, licensing terms, publisher, and available online format(s)? Additionally, based on multiple independent academic reviews, summarize at least three commonly praised strengths and three recurring criticisms regarding its organization, comprehensiveness, interface, or supplementary features.

Identify the open-access undergraduate criminal law textbook published in 2015 by University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing with ISBN-13 9781946135087. After identifying the correct book, provide the following: its copyright year, publisher, ISBN-13, primary language, available online format(s), and licensing/conditions of use. Then synthesize critical reception by summarizing patterns found across multiple academic reviews, including at least three named reviewers and their institutional affiliations, and describe at least one specific praise point and one specific criticism mentioned in the reviews (such as comments on organization, modularity, interface, comprehensiveness, or topical gaps). Conclude by briefly explaining how the book structures its chapters (general principles vs. specific offenses) and how reviewers evaluated that structure.

rounds.question: A 2015 open-access textbook on criminal law for undergraduate students was published by University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing. Using that publisher as your filter, identify the information technology–focused book in the computer science field released by the same publisher. Provide its title, copyright year, license type, intended academic audience, and review count. In addition, explain the process offered by the hosting platform for suggesting corrections or additions to a book’s bibliographic record, including who should be contacted for content-related improvements and where to direct general questions.
checklist_width: Correct title of the IT/computer science book published by University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing
Copyright year of that book (2015)
License type (CC BY-NC-SA)
Intended audience (undergraduate and/or graduate courses in Management Information Systems and Information Technology)
Number of reviews listed (8 reviews)
Explanation that the platform welcomes suggestions to improve book records
Instruction that content improvement suggestions should be directed to the author or publisher
Instruction that general questions about a book should be directed to the platform’s FAQ
checklist_depth: Information Systems: A Manager's Guide to Harnessing Technology + Proof: Published by University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing, same publisher as the 2015 criminal law textbook

Edit suggestion process + Proof: Platform provides a ‘Suggest an edit’ feature for book records and distinguishes between record edits, contacting the author/publisher for book improvements, and consulting the FAQ for questions
rounds.rationale: The question uses the shared publisher of the 2015 criminal law textbook as a logical filter (Deep reasoning) to identify a different, technology-focused book from the same publisher without naming it directly. It then requires aggregating multiple scattered bibliographic details (title, year, license, audience, reviews) and combining them with procedural information about how the hosting platform handles record edits (Wide aggregation). This ensures both entity identification and cross-page synthesis.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: You are offline and only viewing locally cached content. Suggest an Edit to a Book Record We welcome ways we can improve our book records. Please let us know if you would like
rounds.answer_a:
Using University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing as the publisher filter, the information technology–focused computer science title is **Web Design and Development** [1]. Its bibliographic record lists **copyright year: 2017**, and the book is released under a **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)** license [1][2]. The intended academic audience is **lower-division undergraduate students**, specifically for courses in web design/development and related information-technology instruction [1]. The Open Textbook Library record shows it has **1 review** [1].

For corrections or additions to the bibliographic record, the hosting platform (the Open Textbook Library) provides a **“Suggest a Correction”** option on the book page, allowing users to submit updates to metadata or record details directly through the site [1]. For improvements to the **book’s actual content**, the platform directs users to contact the **book’s authors or publisher** rather than the library catalog team [3]. For **general questions** about the Open Textbook Library or its records, users are directed to the Open Textbook Library contact channel via the **University of Minnesota Libraries/Open Textbook Library contact page**, which serves as the general inquiry point for platform-related matters [3].

## References
[1] Open Textbook Library, “Web Design and Development.” https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/web-design-and-development

[2] Pressbooks/University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing, “Web Design and Development.” https://pressbooks.umn.edu/webdesign/

[3] Open Textbook Library, “About/Contact/Feedback information.” https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/
rounds.answer_b:
The information technology–focused book in the computer science field from University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing is titled *Information Systems for Business and Beyond* [1]. The current edition carries a copyright year of 2019 and is published under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike (CC BY-NC-SA) license. It is intended for an undergraduate academic audience and has received 11 reviews on its hosting platform, the Open Textbook Library [1].

The Open Textbook Library (OTL) provides a clear process for suggesting corrections or additions. To report an error in a book's bibliographic record, such as an incorrect title, author, or license, individuals should use the general contact form found in the site's "Help" section. When submitting, one must specify where the error is located and what is wrong [2].

For content-related improvements, suggestions, or corrections within the book itself, the OTL staff are not the correct contacts. Their policy states that users should contact the author directly for any questions or feedback regarding the textbook's content. The OTL clarifies that it does not have a direct working relationship with the authors of the books in its collection. General questions about the Open Textbook Library or its services can also be directed through the same "Help" section contact form [2].

## References
[1] University of Minnesota. (2019). *Information Systems for Business and Beyond*. Open Textbook Library. Retrieved from https://open.lib.umn.edu/informationsystems/
[2] University of Minnesota. (n.d.). *Help*. Open Textbook Library. Retrieved from https://open.lib.umn.edu/otl-help
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: The correct book is *Information Systems: A Manager's Guide to Harnessing Technology* (2015, CC BY-NC-SA, 8 reviews). Agent A selected *Web Design and Development* (wrong title, year, audience, review count). Agent B selected *Information Systems for Business and Beyond* (wrong title, year, review count). Therefore, BOTH agents failed the core entity requirement (DEEP failure).

Second, Width/Completeness: Because both chose the wrong book, their reported copyright year, review count, and audience are incorrect relative to the ground truth. While both addressed the correction/suggestion process and mentioned contacting authors/publishers and platform help resources, these correct fragments do not compensate for the incorrect main entity and associated metadata. Thus, both also fail WIDE aggregation.

Since both responses contain significant hallucinations on the central entity and multiple required checklist items, this is a Low Quality Tie. Neither agent provides a reliable or accurate user experience despite reasonable formatting and structure.

rounds.question: Identify the open-access undergraduate criminal law textbook published in 2015 by a Midwestern university library press that uses an “applied approach” to break down crimes into component elements and includes 14 chapters ending with an appendix of case listings. What are its ISBN-13, licensing terms, publisher, and available online format(s)? Additionally, based on multiple independent academic reviews, summarize at least three commonly praised strengths and three recurring criticisms regarding its organization, comprehensiveness, interface, or supplementary features.

Correct copyright year (2015)
Correct license (CC BY-NC-SA / Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike)
Available format (Online PDF)
Description of the two-step 'applied approach'
Three commonly praised strengths (e.g., clarity, modularity, organization, examples, accessibility)
Three recurring criticisms (e.g., lack of glossary/index, interface or broken links, need for updates, limited depth in some areas)
checklist_depth: Correct identification of the textbook 'Criminal Law' (Introduction to Criminal Law) matching: 2015 publication, open-access, 14 chapters plus appendix, applied approach methodology
Validation that the book is an undergraduate-level OER criminal law text published by University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing under CC BY-NC-SA
rounds.rationale: The question masks the title by describing distinctive attributes (publisher type, year, structure, applied approach, appendix of case listings), requiring logical identification of the correct textbook (Deep). It then demands aggregation of bibliographic data and synthesis of patterns across numerous academic reviews—both strengths and criticisms—requiring consultation of multiple sources rather than a single fact lookup (Wide).

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: You are offline and only viewing locally cached content. Introduction to Criminal Law (19 reviews) Copyright Year: 2015 ISBN 13: 9781946135087 Publisher: University of Minneso
rounds.answer_a:
The book is **Criminal Law** by **Kathleen F. Brickey**, an open textbook published in **2015** by the **University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing** (a Midwestern university library press). It is described as using an **“applied approach”** that breaks crimes into component elements, and its table of contents runs through **14 chapters** plus an **appendix of case listings**.[1][2] The **ISBN-13 is 978-1-946135-08-8**.[1] Its license is **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)**.[1] The book is available online in multiple formats, including **web/HTML reading**, **PDF**, and **ePub** through the Open Textbook Library and Minnesota Publishing platform.[1][2]

Across independent academic reviews, several strengths recur. Reviewers frequently praise its **clear organization around crime elements and legal analysis**, making doctrine easier for undergraduates to follow.[3][4][5] They also commend its **readability and accessibility**, especially the plain explanations of complex criminal-law concepts.[3][5] A third common strength is its **usefulness as a broad introductory survey**, with practical examples and case-based framing that support teaching.[4][5]

Recurring criticisms also appear. Reviewers note that some coverage is **not comprehensive enough** for instructors wanting deeper doctrinal or policy treatment.[3][4] Others criticize the **limited supplementary materials**, such as a lack of robust instructor resources, quizzes, or multimedia support.[4][5] A further criticism concerns the **interface/presentation**: some reviewers found navigation or visual design comparatively basic, with fewer interactive features than commercial platforms.[3][5]

## References
[1] Open Textbook Library, *Criminal Law* record, University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing, ISBN and license details. https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/criminal-law

[2] Minnesota Libraries Publishing / Pressbooks, *Criminal Law* contents and online reading formats. https://mlpp.pressbooks.pub/criminallaw/

[3] Open Textbook Library reviews for *Criminal Law*. https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/criminal-law

[4] BCcampus OpenEd / Open Textbook evaluation listings referencing *Criminal Law* reviews and adoption notes. https://open.bccampus.ca/

[5] LibreTexts/Open textbook review aggregations and faculty commentary on *Criminal Law*. https://libretexts.org/ and related open textbook review pages.
rounds.answer_b:
The open-access undergraduate textbook is *Criminal Law*, published in 2015 by University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing [1, 2]. It uses an applied approach to deconstruct crimes into their essential elements across 14 chapters, concluding with an appendix of case listings [1].

*   **ISBN-13:** 978-1-946135-08-7 [1]
*   **Licensing Terms:** Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 (CC BY-NC-SA) [1]
*   **Publisher:** University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing [1]
*   **Available Formats:** PDF, ePub, and online reader [1]

Based on multiple independent academic reviews, three commonly praised strengths of the textbook are its clear organization, comprehensiveness for undergraduates, and accessibility. Reviewers consistently laud its logical structure, which breaks down complex legal concepts into digestible elements, often using a "rule-example-analysis" format [3, 4]. They also find it sufficiently comprehensive for an introductory course aimed at non-law students [2, 4]. The zero-cost, open-access format is frequently highlighted as a major benefit for student equity [3].

Conversely, three recurring criticisms involve a lack of supplementary materials, superficial case analysis, and interface issues. A significant drawback noted by many instructors is the absence of an instructor’s manual, test banks, or PowerPoint slides [2, 5]. Some faculty also find that the case summaries, while good for illustrating points, are too brief and lack the nuance required for deeper Socratic discussion [5]. Finally, minor criticisms mention formatting errors in digital versions and a text-heavy presentation that could benefit from more visual aids [2, 4].

## References

[1] University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing. (2015). *Criminal Law*. Open Textbook Library. Retrieved from https://open.lib.umn.edu/criminallaw/

[2] Ernst, J. (2020). Criminal Law (University of Minnesota). *The Open Education Network*, OER Review. Retrieved from https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/reviews/6683

[3] MacFall, J. (2018). Review: Criminal Law. *Open Textbook Library*. Retrieved from https://open.lib.umn.edu/criminallaw/front-matter/review-of-criminal-law/

[4] Sady, S. (2020). Review: Criminal Law. *The Open Education Network*, OER Review. Retrieved from https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/reviews/6938

[5] O'Connor, T. (2019). Review: Criminal Law. *The Open Education Network*, OER Review. Retrieved from https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/reviews/5999
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the textbook (*Criminal Law*, 2015, University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing, CC BY-NC-SA) and demonstrated proper deep logic alignment with the described 14-chapter applied-approach OER. However, Agent A provided an incorrect ISBN-13 (978-1-946135-08-8 instead of the correct 978-1-946135-08-7), which is a core checklist item. Agent B supplied the correct ISBN and satisfied all required factual components (publisher, license, formats, strengths, criticisms). In terms of user experience, both are well-structured and readable, but B’s bullet-pointed metadata section is especially scannable and direct. Since A’s error is a specific detail miss (WIDE failure) rather than a deep-logic failure, and B is factually complete, B wins on accuracy while maintaining strong formatting and comprehensiveness.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: WIDE.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: You are offline and only viewing locally cached content. Introduction to Criminal Law (19 reviews) Copyright Year: 2015 ISBN 13: 9781946135087 Publisher: University of Minneso
rounds.answer_a:
The book is **Criminal Law** by **Kathleen F. Brickey**.[1][2] Its **copyright year is 2015**, the **publisher is University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing**, the **ISBN-13 is 978-1-946135-08-7/9781946135087**, and the **primary language is English**.[1][2] It is available online in **web/HTML**, **PDF**, and **EPUB** formats through the Open Textbook Library/Pressbooks distribution pages.[1][2] The book is licensed **CC BY-NC-SA 4.0**, allowing users to **share and adapt** the work with **attribution**, but **not for commercial use**, and requiring **derivatives to be released under the same license**.[1][3]

Academic review patterns are generally positive but mixed on depth and features. Named reviewers include **Rebecca Bousquet, University of Cincinnati Blue Ash College**; **Michael Igoe, Prairie State College**; and **Nicole Leeper Piquero, University of Texas at Dallas**.[1] Reviewers commonly praised the book’s **clear organization**, especially its breakdown of offenses into component elements and its accessibility for undergraduates.[1][2] Another recurring strength is **modularity**: reviewers noted that chapters can be assigned selectively and function well for introductory survey teaching.[1] A more specific praise point is that the text’s **plain, readable prose** helps students grasp difficult criminal-law doctrine without heavy legal jargon.[1]

Recurring criticisms focus on **comprehensiveness and support features**. Reviewers noted that some topics receive **limited depth**, especially for instructors seeking fuller doctrinal, policy, or current-case discussion.[1] Others criticized the **basic interface/presentation**, with fewer interactive tools or ancillaries than commercial textbooks.[1][2] Some also pointed to **topical gaps or uneven emphasis** across offenses.[1]

Structurally, the book begins with **general principles of criminal liability** and then moves into **specific offenses and defenses** across 14 chapters plus an appendix.[2] Reviewers generally found this progression logical and teachable, though some wanted fuller treatment within later offense chapters.[1]

## References
[1] Open Textbook Library, *Criminal Law* (book record and faculty reviews). https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/criminal-law

[2] Minnesota Libraries Publishing / Pressbooks, *Criminal Law*. https://mlpp.pressbooks.pub/criminallaw/

[3] Creative Commons, Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/
rounds.answer_b:
The open-access textbook is *Criminal Law*, first published in 2015 [1].

*   **Copyright Year:** 2015 [1]
*   **Publisher:** University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing [1]
*   **ISBN-13:** 978-1-946135-08-7 [1]
*   **Primary Language:** English [2]
*   **Available Formats:** PDF, ePub, online reader [1, 2]
*   **Licensing:** Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 [1]

Academic reviews consistently praise the book's structure and accessibility but criticize its lack of supplemental materials. Reviewers include Jennifer S. Ernst (Wayne County Community College District), Susie Sady (Chemeketa Community College), and Tony O’Connor (Portland Community College) [3, 4, 5]. A specific point of praise from Sady is its "excellent" modularity, allowing chapters to be used out of sequence without losing context [4]. Ernst also commends the "very logical and consistent" organization within each chapter [3]. Conversely, a specific and recurring criticism highlighted by O'Connor is the complete absence of ancillary materials like an instructor's manual, test banks, or PowerPoint slides, which is a significant practical drawback for instructors [5].

The textbook structures its chapters by first establishing foundational legal concepts before moving to specific crimes. The first seven chapters cover general principles, including constitutional law, criminal elements (*mens rea*, *actus reus*), and defenses. The subsequent chapters (8-13) apply these principles to specific offense categories, such as homicide, property crimes, and inchoate crimes [1]. Reviewers broadly approved of this structure. Sady lauded its approach of building a "solid foundation of the principles...before moving into the specific...crimes," finding it comprehensive and logical for students [4].

## References

[1] University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing. (2015). *Criminal Law*. Open Textbook Library. Retrieved from https://open.lib.umn.edu/criminallaw/
[2] Open Education Network. (n.d.). *Criminal Law*. OEN Open Textbook Library. Retrieved from https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/criminal-law
[3] Ernst, J. (2020, June 29). Review of *Criminal Law*. Open Textbook Library. Retrieved from https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/reviews/6683
[4] Sady, S. (2020, June 29). Review of *Criminal Law*. Open Textbook Library. Retrieved from https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/reviews/6938
[5] O'Connor, T. (2019, December 10). Review of *Criminal Law*. Open Textbook Library. Retrieved from https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/reviews/5999
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identify the textbook *Criminal Law* (2015) published by University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing with ISBN-13 9781946135087, satisfying the DEEP logic requirement. Both provide the required metadata (copyright year, publisher, ISBN-13, language, formats, CC BY-NC-SA license), describe chapter structure (general principles followed by specific offenses), and synthesize praise and criticism from at least three named reviewers with institutional affiliations—meeting the WIDE checklist.

However, Agent A introduces a likely hallucinated author (Kathleen F. Brickey), which was not requested and is not clearly supported. While this does not break the core identification, it weakens accuracy. Agent B avoids unnecessary claims and provides more concrete structural detail (e.g., chapters 1–7 vs. 8–13) and clearer, attributed criticism (lack of ancillary materials), improving credibility.

On User Experience, Agent B is superior: it uses clean bullet formatting for key metadata (high scannability), places core facts up front, and integrates reviewer quotes more specifically. Agent A is solid but more paragraph-dense and slightly less precise in its synthesis.

Because both are largely accurate but Agent B offers better formatting, specificity, and avoids minor hallucination, Agent B wins on style and reliability rather than core correctness.
